title: Soft Machine
description: Soft Machine is a novel by William S. Burroughs. It was unrestricted by the OFLC in 2014 after Auckland Council made a request for reconsideration.
descriptionParagraphs: Soft Machine is a novel by William S. Burroughs. It was unrestricted by the OFLC in 2014 after Auckland Council made a request for reconsideration.

The surviving record is interesting because it shows how even ordinary-looking books can acquire a charged political afterlife. As a novel, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in New Zealand. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: Given an R18 classification in 1971. It was unrestricted by the OFLC in 2014 after Auckland Council made a request for reconsideration. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

overview.englishDescription: Soft Machine is a novel by William S. Burroughs. It was unrestricted by the OFLC in 2014 after Auckland Council made a request for reconsideration.
banningParagraphs: Soft Machine entered censorship debates as a novel associated with controversy, publication history, and state scrutiny. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around political sensitivity.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1971-2014 in New Zealand, where Indecent Publications Tribunal classified, prohibited, or restricted. Given an R18 classification in 1971. It was unrestricted by the OFLC in 2014 after Auckland Council made a request for reconsideration. Given an R18 classification in 1971. It was unrestricted by the OFLC in 2014 after Auckland Council made a request for reconsideration.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
